steps.name: warnings[] contract normalization + contract-change to api-spec §3
Script node (script.generate prompt, §6) + number-set validator
steps.last_error: initial word-tokenizer used a hand-rolled Vietnamese letter char class that silently dropped diacritics like ẩ/ộ, breaking word-number parsing -- fixed by switching to Unicode \w+ (see decisions[])

steps.name: WARN-claim disclosure enforcement (BR-4)
steps.last_error: initial keyword-overlap check (single shared word) false-positived on common Vietnamese function words (không/dung) -- fixed with a stopword list + require >=2 overlapping keywords (see decisions[])

steps.name: Two sub-step approvals + PUT version manual edit + parent lineage (AC5)
Wire remaining tests + verify all Acceptance Criteria

decisions.decision: Task file's 'outline 7 phần' AC1 interpreted as the 7 always-present narrative sections (hook/introduction/problem/solution/demo/conclusion/cta), excluding the 8th 'controversy' field which prompts.md §5 itself defines as optional/nullable ('KHÔNG bịa tranh cãi nếu các nguồn đồng thuận').
decisions.why: docs/specs/prompts.md §5's actual JSON schema has 8 keys; reconciling the task file's '7 phần' wording against the authoritative prompt contract rather than either redesigning the prompt or leaving an unresolved contradiction -- reversible/locally-scoped naming/count judgment call.

decisions.decision: script content gets a new source_outline_version field (in content JSONB, not StepVersion.parent_version) to carry cross-step lineage for AC5.
decisions.why: StepVersion.parent_version (app/models/step_version.py) is intra-step only (used for restore-chain lineage within the same step name) -- there is no existing DB field for 'which outline version was this script generated from'. Adding it inside content avoids a schema migration.

decisions.decision: BR-1 source filtering uses a keyword-overlap heuristic (>=4-char word overlap between FAIL claim text and source text), not the claim's evidence[].source_id links.
decisions.why: evidence links tell us which sources were *checked* against a claim, not which source(s) actually authored the failed wording -- a source can support one claim and contradict another. A distinctive-keyword heuristic is a pragmatic, testable stand-in; revisit if a real cross-reference field is added later.

decisions.decision: Number-set normalizer's Vietnamese word tokenizer switched from a hand-rolled ASCII+partial-diacritic character class to Python's Unicode-aware \w+.
decisions.why: The original class was missing several diacritics (ẩ, ộ, ố...), silently truncating words like 'phẩy'->'ph'+'y' and 'một'->nothing, which broke decimal detection entirely. Caught by the task's own highest-scrutiny number-set unit test before it shipped.

decisions.decision: A bare single spelled-out digit-word (e.g. 'năm' alone) is NOT counted as a number unless part of a multi-word compound or a 'phẩy' decimal continuation.
decisions.why: 'năm' is a genuine Vietnamese homograph (year vs. digit 5), 'một' is also an article/number -- treating every lone occurrence as a number produced false positives (e.g. 'ra mắt năm 2026' reads 'năm' as year, not 5), which surfaced as a failing subset-check test. Multi-word compounds ('chín mươi hai') and phẩy-continuations are unambiguous, so those are still counted.

decisions.decision: WARN-claim 'used in script' detection requires >=2 overlapping distinctive (len>=4) keywords between claim and sentence (or all of them if the claim has fewer than 2), plus an added stopword list for common Vietnamese connector words.
decisions.why: A single shared word (e.g. 'không'/'dung', both very common) produced a false positive on an unrelated-claim test case. Requiring 2+ genuinely distinctive keywords is a much stronger 'this sentence is about this claim' signal than any single word.
